data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a military-technological cascade caused by the rapid diffusion of iron weaponry and the 'swarm' tactics of infantry, which democratized violence and overwhelmed the bronze-age chariot-based aristocracies that had previously maintained a stable balance of power.", "mechanism": "The centuries of stability were maintained by a narrow elite monopoly on expensive bronze weapons and chariot warfare, which limited the scale of conflict. The gradual spread of iron smelting technology from the north disrupted this equilibrium. Iron ore was abundant and cheap, allowing for the mass arming of infantry. Simultaneously, new infantry tactics (javelin-armed skirmishers, massed swordsmen) proved highly effective against chariot formations on broken terrain. This combination allowed peripheral or lower-status groups to successfully challenge and annihilate the established palace-warrior elites in a short, violent period. The 'Sea Peoples' were the most successful of these newly armed, mobile infantry confederations.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Empire", "Mycenaean Greece", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Metallurgical analysis of weapons from the destruction layers shows that the attackers' weapons were overwhelmingly made of bronze, identical in composition to those of the defenders.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Contemporary diplomatic correspondence (e.g., Amarna/Ugarit letters) from the period shows no awareness of or concern about new military formations or iron-armed adversaries.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because its centralized state under Ramesses III could quickly adopt and mass-produce iron weapons for its own infantry and mercenaries, and its geography (narrow Delta) neutralized the swarm advantage, as shown in the Medinet Habu reliefs. This account makes the collapse a true military revolution, not just a migration."}
